data.title: Widodo hails Southeast Asia’s rise as ‘global force’ in entrepreneurship
data.description:
Across Southeast Asia, millions of young people are launching start-ups and driving rapid digitalisation, with new unicorns emerging in a sign that the region is becoming a global force in entrepreneurship, according to former Indonesian president Joko Widodo.
Speaking at the closing of the Bloomberg New Economy Forum on Friday, Widodo said the next unicorn – meaning a privately held start-up valued at more than US$1 billion – might emerge not from Silicon Valley or Shenzhen but from Jakarta, Singapore, Bangkok, Kuala Lumpur, Manila or Hanoi.
“Southeast Asia is no longer just a market, it is becoming a global force,” he said.
He urged countries to ready themselves for what he called an “intelligent economy” by prioritising the free flow of data and accelerating the adoption of technologies such as artificial intelligence, arguing that these would reshape production, strategy and ultimately income levels.
According to equity management software firm Eqvista, in May this year there were more than 1,550 unicorn companies globally and 44 were based in Southeast Asia.
Singapore leads with 22 unicorn start-ups, followed by Indonesia with 10 and Vietnam with five.


Widodo said Indonesia’s experience showed how regulatory clarity and supportive innovation policies could allow local companies to thrive.
Looking ahead, he rejected the idea that AI would wipe out jobs, provided governments ensured workers had the skills to use the technology.
“I really disagree that job opportunities will disappear. In fact, I believe that there will be more jobs and more opportunities to come, if we make sure our people are ready for them,” said Widodo, who served as Indonesian president for a decade from 2014.
He stressed the need for workers to study AI, coding, algorithms and machine learning, describing technological literacy as “fundamental” as national competitiveness increasingly depends on how effectively societies can use data.
International institutions like the International Monetary Fund and the World Bank would have to redefine their financial instruments and systems, and the World Trade Organization would also need to update its rules on trade and tariffs, he said without elaborating.

During Widodo’s tenure, Indonesia’s GDP growth consistently hovered at about 5 per cent, while the country also saw a boost in infrastructure in the form of new airports, ports, dams and toll roads.
“Indonesia’s journey shows that transformation needs courage, persistence and belief. With [a] strong foundation, innovation and global collaboration, Indonesia and Southeast Asia will continue to grow in this new era,” Widodo said.
“In this new economy, the intelligent economy, nations, companies and societies that can integrate intelligence into governance, industries and social systems will grow better and faster.”
Widodo’s call for an “intelligent economy” comes at a moment when Southeast Asia’s start-up sector is facing its harshest funding climate in a decade.
Venture capital funding in Asia totalled just US$51.2 billion in the first nine months of this year, according to accounting firm KPMG. Data from business analytics firm Crunchbase reveals a broader trend: funding hit a decade low of US$65.8 billion last year, a far cry from the record US$194 billion raised in 2021.

data.title: 18 dead in Pakistan glue factory explosion amid safety concerns: ‘like an earthquake’
data.description:
A boiler at a glue-making factory in eastern Pakistan exploded on Friday, killing at least 18 people and injuring 21 others. The force of the blast destroyed the factory building and damaged several nearby homes, igniting a huge fire and spreading panic through the area, officials said.
Police said they arrested the factory manager and were looking for the factory owner, who fled shortly after the explosion at the industrial facility in Faisalabad, a city in Punjab province.
The cause of the explosion was not immediately known, according to Punjab police chief Usman Anwar. Police later said a gas leakage may have caused it.
It was not clear how many of the 18 dead were factory workers and how many were local residents. Earlier reports said at least 15 workers were killed.
Local administrator Raja Jahangir said an investigation was under way to determine how construction permits allowed the factory to be built in a residential area of Faisalabad, in violation of building laws.

The explosion “completely flattened” the factory, Jahangir said, adding that rescuers spent hours pulling bodies and survivors out of the rubble. Several of the injured were in critical condition.
Witnesses said the blast was so powerful that it collapsed nearby homes and hurled debris across the streets.
Punjab Chief Minister Maryam Nawaz Sharif expressed her condolences and ordered authorities to ensure the best possible medical care for the injured.
Muhammad Iqbal, one of the injured at the local hospital, said it felt “like an earthquake” and that “roofs and walls of nearby houses collapsed”. He said his wife and son were also injured, but they were in a stable condition.
Roofs and walls of nearby houses collapsed
Muhammad Iqbal, one of the injured
Nadeem Zafar, 45, said he rushed out of his home after hearing a deafening boom and described the scene of chaos that ensued.
“I saw flames and thick smoke rising,” he said over the phone. “People were screaming, running in every direction, calling for help.”
Ambulances and rescuers arrived quickly and began pulling bodies out of the rubble in the Malikpur neighbourhood, he said, adding that many around him initially thought it was a bomb or some sort of projectile.
Poor safety standards are a frequent cause of industrial accidents and factory fires in Pakistan.
Last year, a dozen workers were injured in a similar boiler explosion at a textile mill in Faisalabad. Also last week, a blast at a firecrackers factory in Pakistan’s port city of Karachi killed four people.

data.title: San Francisco Opera’s The Monkey King is a thrilling landmark in cultural exchange
data.description:
San Francisco Opera’s premiere of The Monkey King is a visually enchanting retelling of the timeless Chinese folk tale and a thrilling landmark in cultural exchange.
With music by Huang Ruo and libretto by David Henry Hwang, this new opera follows the mischievous antics of Sun Wukong, the macaque born from a rock, who discovers this simple truth on his path towards enlightenment and immortality: “You cannot find the land of bliss with a leap and a bound.”
The sets and puppetry by Basil Twist are a wonder and delight from start to finish.
A staggering 4.1 kilometres (2.6 miles) of silk is used to create rippling visions of the undersea kingdom, of flights through the heavens, and of fantastical encounters with magic white horses, hovering jellyfish and moving mountains.
Layers of transparent curtains sweep across the stage, revealing ever more magical worlds of light and colour.
The Monkey character has three forms: a singer in Kang Wang, a puppet who can fly through the air, and a nimble dancer, Huiwang Zhang. Via clever stagecraft, they seamlessly take turns depicting the many aspects of Sun Wukong.

The well-cast singers have glorious voices and sing impeccably. Soprano Meigui Zhang as the goddess Guanyin, suspended in a glowing bubble high above the stage for most of the performance, has a heavenly, crystalline sound. Her calm, wise and benevolent demeanour is perfect for the role.
Tenor Konu Kim as the Jade Emperor struts and sings convincingly. Peixin Chen, as the Supreme Sage Laojun, impresses with his dark, low notes.
Tenor Kang Wang is the ideal Monkey King. He embodies the feisty spirit of Sun Wukong, twirling a magic staff and doing lively leaps and spins. His voice has golden, ringing high notes and a compelling, expressive midrange that is a delight to the ears.
The music does not have set “numbers” but is composed with long lines in the manner established by the German composer Wagner. It is not an opera with musical “hooks” or catchy repeated melodic ideas. It is nevertheless lyrical, but often with a solemn, abstract tone, reminiscent of the chanting of a Greek chorus.
The harmonic language is modern with a persistent level of subtle dissonance, except for certain moments when the clouds part, so to speak, and the discord gives way to concord to great effect.
The pacing is quite varied. Director Diane Paulus tells the story clearly and vividly – not an easy trick for a story combining a superhero monkey with a deeper Buddhist message.

There are lively percussive sections in the dramatic fight and action scenes, including woodblocks and Chinese opera gongs with their strident rising pitch.
The chorus of monkeys has sprightly, chattering music. The orchestra plays lovely, intricate, minimalist-inspired textures to depict flying through the heavens or the undersea realm. In a climactic scene, the orchestra rises to terrifying power as Buddha’s stone hand closes around the Monkey King.
At times, the impact of the singing is limited because the orchestra does not fully support the voices. The singers work hard to express the poignant words with their ringing high notes, but they are acoustically at odds with the orchestra. The moments when they are in clear harmony together are electrifying.
Monkey’s hard-won spiritual growth is movingly depicted, especially near the end when he arrives at what he thinks is the Land of Bliss, or true enlightenment.
Here, the music has a more traditional Chinese sound with the open harmonies of the pentatonic scale, as in the black keys on the piano. It sets the perfect tone for Monkey’s glimpse of what it means to be humble and let go of the ego.
Also near the end, Guanyin has a beautiful passage with solo strings, and her tones ring out with purity as she sings about “relieving the suffering of all creatures regardless of station”.

This tale is so appealing – no wonder a Monkey King video game is so popular – that it is the perfect introduction to Chinese culture for general audiences.
For children, the appeal might be martial arts and comical mischief. For adults, there is a timeless message of compassion. This opera is a welcome landmark moment of connection across cultures: a window into deep Chinese culture, presented by authentic Chinese voices and made accessible to all.
“The Monkey King” by Huang Ruo and David Henry Hwang, San Francisco Opera, until November 30. Reviewed: November 16. On-demand “live stream” viewing online is available from November 23-25.

data.title: Stunning water stage provides backdrop to National Games closing ceremony
data.description:
Honestly, at some point this week while trying to decide how to start this blog, the lyrics from My Way entered my head.
I don’t know if you’ve ever read them properly, but I never used to consider the song particularly morbid, so another learning opportunity there.
Anyway, the sentiment kind of stands. At the end of two action-packed weeks, we’re finally at the end of the National Games and what a fortnight it’s been.
The closing ceremony promises to be one for the ages too, so strap in and let’s see what the final few hours hold.

data.title: Chinese vice-premier urges manufacturing upgrades, trade diversification in policy push
data.description:
Vice-Premier He Lifeng has emphasised the need to diversify China’s export markets and sustain trade growth, while urging local-level officials to empower enterprises and enhance policy support to strengthen manufacturing.
His remarks came during a three-day inspection tour that concluded on Thursday in the central provinces of Hubei and Hunan, where he learned about the operations of local trade logistics firms, export-oriented manufacturers and consumer-sector companies, according to Xinhua.
“In the face of a severe and complex external environment, China’s foreign trade has withstood pressure and maintained steady growth, providing strong support for the domestic economy,” he said.
He called for the support of cross-border e-commerce, overseas warehouse expansion, and continued market diversification to maintain stable trade growth.
The high-profile tour was made as China’s exports – a key driver of the world’s second-largest economy – have faced mounting pressure this year, particularly amid heightened trade tensions with the United States.
During his inspection, He urged authorities to “resolutely remove bottlenecks hindering the development of a unified national market” – reiterating a policy priority recently emphasised by President Xi Jinping as leadership aims to build a resilient domestic market to counter external uncertainties.


The vice-premier highlighted the importance of accelerating the development of a modern logistics system to reduce costs across the economy. And he emphasised that regions must adapt to the latest technological and industrial shifts, urging local officials to capitalise on the role of enterprises while strengthening policy and financial support to promote “high-quality growth” in manufacturing.
He also stressed the need for breakthroughs in key technologies, and for accelerating progress in “making production and supply chains more independent and controllable”.
Beijing has prioritised boosting technological self-reliance and deepening manufacturing capabilities, as outlined in a communique released after last month’s fourth plenum of the Communist Party’s Central Committee, which outlined policy priorities for the next five years.
The vice-premier also addressed the persistent issue of cutthroat, low-quality price competition – known as “involution” – in sectors ranging from electric vehicles to food delivery. He reiterated the need for authorities to crack down on such detrimental business practices while guiding export-oriented firms to expand abroad in a manner that is orderly and rational.
Local-level officials must remain confident, He said, while “confronting and addressing” the challenges that enterprises encounter.
“[We should] decisively meet this year’s economic targets and secure a smooth conclusion to the 14th five-year plan and a solid start to the 15th,” he added, referring to the next entry in a line of expansive blueprints that have set the tone for the country’s development over more than seven decades.

data.title: Japanese leader says she wants better ties with China but doesn’t withdraw Taiwan comments
data.description:
Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi said on Friday the country had been “consistent” over Taiwan but insisted she wanted better relations with China.
Beijing has insisted she must retract her recent comments that Japanese troops might intervene in a conflict over the island because it could be a “survival-threatening situation”.
But when asked if she would do so, she did not answer directly but said Tokyo would “make a comprehensive judgment based on all available information regarding a situation threatening Japan’s existence”.
Speaking to reporters before she left for the Group of 20 summit in South Africa, she added: “I myself have repeatedly stated this position in my responses. The government’s stance remains consistent.”
Takaichi added that she still hoped for better relations with China. She said that when she met Chinese President Xi Jinping at the recent Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation summit in South Korea, they had “confirmed the broad direction of comprehensively advancing our strategic and mutually beneficial relationship and building a constructive and stable relationship”.
She added: “There has been no change whatsoever in this stance.”
As well as calling on her to withdraw the comments, Beijing has also asked Tokyo to stick to its political commitments.


It regarded them as meddling in its internal affairs, and was concerned that they could mark a departure from Japan’s strategic ambiguity on how it would react to a conflict in the Taiwan Strait.
On Friday, Chinese foreign ministry spokeswoman Mao Ning repeated the demand when asked about Takaichi’s comments about wanting better relations.
She said if that really was the case, Japan should “immediately retract its erroneous remarks and truly translate its commitments to China into concrete actions”.
Beijing sees Taiwan as part of China and has never ruled out the use of force to reunite it with the mainland.
Most countries, including Japan and its main ally the United States, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state, but Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the island by force and is committed to supplying it with weapons.
Beijing has previously said that Premier Li Qiang, who is representing China at the G20 summit, will not meet Takaichi at the event.
It has also postponed a planned ministerial meeting between China, Japan and South Korea, saying the Japanese leader had “undermined the basis and atmosphere for cooperation” among the three countries.
Beijing has also announced a series of economic countermeasures and threatened further action if there is no retraction.
On Wednesday, it reportedly reimposed a ban on Japanese seafood imports only weeks after lifting it. The previous ban was imposed because of concerns about the release of treated waste water from the Fukushima nuclear power plant.

Beijing has also urged its citizens to avoid travelling to Japan, resulting in the cancellation of about 590,000 flight bookings, aviation analyst Li Hanming said on Friday.
Imports of some Japanese films have also been halted and several concerts cancelled.
Meanwhile, the education ministry has urged students to reconsider any plans to study there.

data.title: Hong Kong seizes fake Gucci, Hermes, other goods worth HK$65 million during major sale
data.description:
Hong Kong customs has seized 120,000 suspected counterfeit items in a month-long operation that targeted cross-border transshipments during a major online shopping festival in mainland China, confiscating goods including fake Gucci handbags, Hermes shoes and latest models of the iPhone.
The Customs and Excise Department revealed on Friday that it conducted a special enforcement operation between October 20 and November 14, during which it solved a total of 29 cases and seized counterfeit goods worth an estimated HK$65 million (US$8.3 million).
“During the ‘Double 11’ shopping festival, with its massive transaction volume, criminals attempted to exploit the anonymity of online transactions to mix counterfeit goods with legitimate e-commerce products, hoping to smuggle them in or out of Hong Kong,” said Senior Inspector Liu Wai-kit of customs’ intellectual property transnational investigation division.
“Through intelligence analysis and risk management, and cooperating with large platforms handling cross-border e-commerce logistics, customs strengthened its inspections and checks.”
Double 11, or Singles’ Day, a major shopping festival in mainland China, falls on November 11 every year and is celebrated by many online shops and e-commerce platforms that roll out discounts to boost sales.
Liu added that the 29 cases involved multiple logistics centres, with preliminary investigations revealing that the seized items were meant to be shipped overseas via Hong Kong, including destinations in the Americas, Europe and the Middle East.
The seized goods covered many categories including clothing, footwear, electronic products and luxury goods.
Customs said the criminals exploited consumers’ desire to buy counterfeit branded goods at lower prices than the originals, and used the popular online shopping festival to push such bogus products.
Liu noted that the operation yielded a large number of counterfeit electronic products and accessories, including the newest models. He explained that compared with large home appliances, small items that carried a high value, such as wireless headphones, were easier to conceal and transport.
Fake luxury goods, such as Gucci handbags and Hermes and Dior shoes and wallets, could yield even greater profit margins.

This lucrative market drove criminals to continuously improve the quality and resemblance of counterfeit products to genuine articles, to meet the demand for high-end luxury goods, according to Liu.
Customs urged the local logistics industry to be vigilant regarding their clients’ goods. If employees suspected that the items they handled were counterfeit, they should file a report with law enforcement authorities.
The department also advised consumers to shop in stores or online shops with a good reputation. If they had doubts about the authenticity of goods, they should first check with the trademark holder or their authorised agent to avoid unnecessary losses.
Under the Trade Descriptions Ordinance, the maximum penalty for importing or exporting any goods using a forged trademark is a fine of HK$500,000 and five years’ imprisonment.

data.title: Nigerian Catholic school pupils abducted by gunmen in second attack in a week
data.description:
Armed gangs have abducted an unknown number of pupils from a Catholic school in central Nigeria, police and a government official said on Friday, in the second such incident in less than a week.
The school kidnappings and an attack on a church earlier in the week come weeks after US President Donald Trump threatened military action over what he described as the targeted killings of Nigeria’s Christians, a narrative rejected by the Nigerian government.
The Niger state government said it had “received with deep sadness the disturbing news of the kidnapping of pupils from St Mary’s School in Agwara local government area”.
“The exact number of abducted pupils is yet to be confirmed as security agencies continue to assess the situation,” Abubakar Usman, the state government secretary, said in a statement.
Local media broadcaster Arise TV said 52 schoolchildren were abducted.

Coming after gunmen stormed a secondary school in Kebbi state in northwestern Nigeria, abducting 25 schoolgirls early on Monday, Friday’s attack further raises alarm over security in Africa’s most populous country.
For years, heavily armed criminal gangs locally known as “bandits” have been intensifying attacks in rural areas of northwest and central Nigeria with little state presence, killing thousands and conducting kidnappings for ransom.
The state government said the school had defied orders to temporarily close all boarding schools in parts of the state following an intelligence report of an “increased threat level” in parts of northern Niger that border Kebbi.
Niger’s state police said its tactical units and the military were deployed to search for the pupils.
Police said they received a report at about 2am that “some armed bandits invaded St Mary’s Private (Catholic) Secondary School … and abducted a yet to be ascertained number of students from the school’s hostel”.
It said security agencies were “combing the forests with a view to rescue the abducted students”.
The federal government said this week that security forces had been placed on high alert.
Favoured tactic
In a separate attack on a church in western Nigeria on Tuesday, gunmen killed two people during a service that was recorded and broadcast online.
The gunmen also kidnapped 38 worshippers from the church and were demanding a ransom of 100 million naira (US$69,000) per worshipper, a church official said on Friday.
As the country grapples with security challenges on several fronts, hostage-taking has spiralled nationwide and become a favoured tactic of bandit gangs and jihadists.

Although bandits have no ideological leanings and are motivated by financial gains, their increasing alliance with jihadists from the northeast has been a source of concern for authorities and security analysts.
Jihadists have for 16 years been waging an insurrection in the northeast with the aim of establishing a caliphate.
The jihadist violence has killed more than 40,000 people and displaced around 2 million in the northeast since it erupted in 2019.
Additional reporting by Associated Press, Reuters

data.title: Chinese commerce minister calls for deeper US-China cooperation on trade
data.description:
Chinese Commerce Minister Wang Wentao called for China and the United States to make joint efforts to manage uncertainties and expand cooperation on trade during a meeting with the US ambassador to China, David Perdue, in Beijing on Thursday.
The two countries should maintain a “correct understanding” of the fact that China and the US must find ways to coexist and that win-win outcomes could be achieved, Wang said, according to a statement released by the commerce ministry after the meeting.
“Economic and trade ties should continue to serve as the ballast and engine of China-US relations, not as a stumbling block or a source of conflict,” Wang said, adding that the two sides should uphold the bilateral economic and trade consultation mechanism put in place earlier this year.
But Wang also expressed concerns over a range of US policies during the meeting, including its unilateral imposition of tariffs, export controls, restrictions on two-way investments, tougher visa screenings and restrictive clauses in agreements with third parties, according to the statement.
He also laid out China’s position in relation to the dispute over Nexperia – a Chinese-owned chip company that was seized by the Dutch government in late September – and exchanged views with Perdue on the implementation of measures agreed during recent bilateral economic and trade consultations, the statement said.
China was willing to work with the US to implement the consensus reached by Chinese President Xi Jinping and US President Donald Trump, Wang said.
After the two leaders met in late October, the two countries pledged to roll back tariffs on each other’s goods and suspend a series of other trade restrictions, with China agreeing to pause its expanded export controls over rare earth minerals.


In return, the US agreed to a year-long delay to its introduction of the “affiliate rule” – a measure announced in late September that extends US export restrictions to any entity at least 50 per cent owned by companies on a US trade blacklist.
The new rule appeared to play a direct role in the Nexperia saga, as the company’s Chinese owner – Wingtech – was added to the US entity list last year. The Hague moved to wrest control of Nexperia from Wingtech the day after Washington adopted the affiliate rule.
China and the Netherlands have been embroiled in a dispute over the company’s fate ever since, with Beijing imposing export restrictions on certain Nexperia components that threatened the chip supplies of European carmakers.
On Wednesday, the Dutch government announced it had lifted the Cold War-era order that allowed it to take control of Nexperia, a move that Beijing welcomed as a “first step in the right direction”.
During a public hearing on Thursday, US lawmakers argued that suspending the affiliate rule could make it easier for China to challenge America’s lead in semiconductor and artificial intelligence technology, as the one-year delay would give Chinese entities time to “create workarounds” to US export controls.
Wang, the Chinese commerce minister, said Beijing and Washington could bring greater stability and certainty to US-China relations and the global economy by working together to address challenges and broaden cooperation.

data.title: China is betting on the K visa as a magnet for top tech talent. Will it be enough?
data.description:
Computer science graduate Alain Saas is hoping a fourth time is the charm to make his China dream come true.
As a young man growing up in the small town of Selestat in eastern France, the distant Asian country seemed magical. But the dream has been elusive and trying to land a job in China has been anything but enchanting.
Over the past 15 years, Saas, who is now in his forties, has tried three times to find a suitable tech job in China. The attempts have been unsuccessful due to reasons ranging from poor timing and low pay to a lack of suitable opportunities in the industry.
But lately he is feeling more optimistic. As China rolls out friendlier visa offers and appears poised to make more technological leaps, Saas is ready to take his fourth shot.
“I am planning to double down on my search efforts next year after reaching HSK5 [working proficiency in Chinese],” Saas said. “The K Visa sounds good, though I may have passed the age limit … and given all that’s happening in technology in China, I’d be very excited to find something.”
China is also doubling down. Amid a global tech war, a race for talent is ramping up, and Beijing has committed to becoming a magnet for the world’s skilled immigrants in its economic and social development blueprint for the next five years.
In the proposals for the country’s 15th five-year plan released last month, the Central Committee of China’s ruling Communist Party announced it would “establish an immigration system for highly skilled talent” and “attract and cultivate outstanding talent from around the world”.
The pledge – an upgrade on the 14th five-year plan’s promise to merely “explore” tech immigration – came around the same time as last month’s launch of the K visa, a streamlined pathway targeting young professionals in majors of science, technology, engineering and mathematics (STEM).
Details of the K visa are still unknown, including the age limit, scope of scientific and technological fields or industries covered, the specific validity period and permitted length of stay, and residential requirements for visa holders.
According to census data, 845,000 foreign nationals lived in mainland China in 2020, accounting for 0.06 per cent of the total population. In contrast, in June this year, 52 million immigrants were living in the United States – about 15 per cent of that country’s population.


As the US imposes US$100,000 fees on new H-1B applicants under the administration of President Donald Trump, Beijing is positioning the K-visa as a lower-barrier alternative, requiring no employer sponsorship and providing potential fast-tracks to permanent residency, though few details are known.
The policy is the centrepiece of China’s broader tech ambitions: to dominate “new quality productive forces” – a phrase coined by President Xi Jinping to stress innovation-driven economic development as the country’s workforce ages and US export curbs bite into chip and AI tool supplies. The future hurdles for research and development have made foreign expertise essential to China’s goals.
In 2024, China had more than 10 million R&D personnel with the total number of “skilled talent” exceeding 220 million, according to official data. The huge reservoir of talent combined with billions of dollars in state funding has sparked a series of technological advances over the past decade, most visibly in green energy, electric vehicles and artificial intelligence.
However, a shortage of high-level talent – such as top tier scientists – remains an obstacle that must be addressed, according to Li Zuojun, a researcher with the Development Research Centre of the State Council.
China should “expand international openness and cooperation in talent”, Li wrote in the official China Economic Times earlier this month. The measures should include attracting global intellectual resources to help China innovate, improving hi-tech talent immigration policies, simplifying work permits and residence visas and creating a “liveable and workable environment” for talent development, he wrote.
However, insiders have warned that China’s immigration apparatus is not ready yet.
David Chew, a Malaysian satellite-tech founder in his mid-thirties who is fluent in Chinese, embodies the ideal candidate. Several years ago, he chose to start his business in Japan after completing his education there and having worked for Japanese companies. “The K visa is a good start,” he said, “but the local ecosystem is not ready to support it”.
To rival the US, China needs “a standardised points system, multi-year certainty, flat tax rates and seed capital,” Chew said.
Without them, he said talent is deterred by having to choose geopolitical sides. “Coming to China to a certain extent means saying bye to the Western world – like picking Huawei over iPhone,” he said.
Chew predicted early winners: foreigners already in China on student visas, able to switch seamlessly, and “with experience already in place”. A second wave, he added, could come via entrepreneurship programmes offering English navigation and start-up grants.
In aerospace, the gap between rhetoric and reality has become stark. An executive at a private aviation start-up in Beijing, who declined to be named so he could comment freely, acknowledged there was demand but cited paralysing secrecy.
“We’re too closed-off; many units are classified,” he said. “We need international exchanges, but we do not dare recruit foreigners out of fear of leaks.”
In recent years, Beijing has tightened scrutiny over businesses by expanding national security laws, which now cover a broader range of activities and apply to both domestic and foreign companies.
Even non-state firms shun outsiders socially and professionally, the executive said. Domestic workers have largely sufficed unless foreigners offer R&D skills that cannot be duplicated. The job market’s cutthroat dynamic means companies are not “desperate enough to take risks”, he said.
China’s urban jobless rate among those aged 16 to 24 eased slightly to 17.7 per cent in September after climbing to 18.9 per cent in August – a 20-month high in the sagging economy.
Joseph Chamie, a demographer and former director of the United Nations’ Population Division, said that while many governments have attempted to limit immigration, reduce the sizes of their foreign-born populations and address domestic unemployment concerns, countries were also seeking to attract hi-tech migrants and benefit from “brain gains”.
China’s K visa programme stands in “stark contrast” to the US move to raise fees for H-1B visas, he said. However, for many hi-tech and highly educated potential migrants, the US remains a more attractive destination than China for various reasons, including language, a culture of immigration, long-term opportunities and lifestyle, Chamie added.
“Nevertheless, China’s increasing efforts to attract STEM talented migrants are likely to achieve some success especially if the US makes it more costly and difficult for foreign skilled migrants,” he said, adding China was likely to achieve “some limited success” in attracting such talent.
Sourabh Gupta, a senior policy specialist with the Institute for China-America Studies in Washington, said: “The intentions are certainly good; whether the policy turns out to be a success is a different matter, and I lean on the sceptical side of things in this regard.”
“By shooting for young, ambitious talent, the opportunity to build long-term collaborative relationships that span borders has now opened up. Done right, the K visa will not only enrich China’s science and technology ecosystem but could perhaps even incubate a rare few next-generation tech superstars of non-Chinese origin,” Gupta said. “It could also potentially transform major Chinese cities into hubs of cosmopolitanism.”
“But the barriers to success are high too. The ‘Great Firewall’ [blocked internet access to the outside world], the ‘996’ work-life balance, the relative lack of English proficiency or even signage in Chinese cities, etc are only the most immediate,” Gupta cautioned.
“On unconventional immigration initiatives such as the K visa, the government needs to adopt a liberal mindset of ‘setting it free’ … Whether this is even possible within the Chinese system remains to be seen,” he said. “There must be trust, too, among foreign STEM talent that their inventions and discoveries will be adequately protected,” he said.
It is not easy for a foreigner to get a visa to live in China. The application process is complex, requiring navigation of local regulations and submission of extensive documentation, including employment contracts and proof of qualifications, all of which are then thoroughly scrutinised by the authorities.
Under China’s immigration regulations, foreigners who have held senior positions – such as deputy general manager or an equivalent role – for at least four consecutive years and have lived in China for at least three years with a good tax record are eligible to apply for permanent residency.
Those who have made significant and outstanding contributions to China or who are urgently needed by the country are also eligible.
In August, Beijing announced a new category of K visas for young science and technology talent from overseas, which took effect on October 1. Eligible applicants include STEM graduates from recognised universities or research institutions worldwide who hold at least a bachelor’s degree or young professionals engaged in relevant education or research at such institutions.
There is no requirement for a Chinese employer or inviter at the application stage. Holders of K visas can be engaged in education, research, cultural exchange, entrepreneurship or business in China.
Ruby Osman, a policy adviser and China expert at the Tony Blair Institute for Global Change, said visas are only one of the obstacles faced by foreign talent: increasing research openness, providing a clearer regulatory environment and tackling language barriers will be essential if China is to become an attractive long-term talent destination.
There is also a domestic trade-off. The “opening up” policy in immigration is politically delicate. Bringing in new talent is a tough sell when China’s own STEM graduates are already struggling to find jobs, Osman said.
“That means Beijing will need to walk a fine line – promoting the K visa as proof of openness abroad while reassuring audiences at home that any impact will be carefully controlled,” Osman said.
The K visa is a step in the right direction but more details on its specific requirements and clarity on permitted activities would be key in determining its success, according to Ioana Kraft, chair of the human resources working group at the European Union Chamber of Commerce in China.
She said that hiring local Chinese workers had become an established practice for Western companies operating in China in recent years.
“Hiring foreign nationals has been deprioritised by many chamber members, a large percentage of whom have taken the decision to localise their workforce,” Kraft said.
“This is partly due to compensation packages for foreign nationals being higher, but also a reflection of the fact that Chinese staff tend to have a better understanding of the China market, while also having a language advantage,” she added.
Back in 2009, Alain Saas’ first attempt to make it to China fizzled after the salary offers did not meet his expectations. In 2015, a visa issue prevented him from attending a face-to-face interview. Then, a global tech giant that had posted a position downsized its China operations. These days, he says his job searches have turned up fewer openings.
“There seem to be no more or very few foreign companies hiring foreigners, few job postings in English, and in general the feeling I get is that the hiring of ‘foreign talent’ is mostly geared towards Chinese returning from studying or living abroad,” he said.
“In G7 countries all jobs are easy to find on [social media], in English, which is very convenient, but China is unfortunately disconnected from that.”
“So I am trying to slowly grow a network in China and hoping someone will eventually be able to connect me to an opportunity. While still working hard on my Chinese, hoping it will eventually pay off,” he said.
